one_line_takeaway: When jailbreak attacks are evaluated against the full input+output content-filtering pipeline rather than the bare model alone, nearly all of them are caught -- pass rates fall from the >90% success rates reported in prior model-only studies to often under 5% -- but the strongest filters achieve this by trading precision for recall, generating substantial false positives on benign prompts containing sensitive terms.
key_finding: Takeaway 1: safety filters are currently 'winning' the arms race -- PromptGuard and O3 block most injected prompts at the input stage (detection rates roughly 70-100%), driving overall pass rates below ~5% in most cases, far lower than the high ASR figures reported in prior jailbreak-only papers. Takeaway 2: attacks that don't explicitly optimize for semantic stealthiness (e.g., TAP and Adaptive Attacks, which report >90% ASR against bare models) are nonetheless easily caught by filters, inflating their apparent real-world threat; semantically stealthy attacks like ReNeLLM persist longer but are still eventually caught by strong filters (PromptGuard, O3); the multi-turn attack Crescendo is also reliably detected despite its gradual escalation strategy. Takeaway 3: detector effectiveness and model vulnerability vary systematically -- O3 (reasoning model used as detector) achieves the lowest pass rates overall; GradSafe underperforms in nearly half of cases because it depends on paired prompt+'Sure...'-response gradient patterns; Llama-2-7B and Llama3.1-8B show low raw ASR (<30% across six attacks) but when they are successfully jailbroken the resulting exchanges tend to be stealthier and harder for filters to catch. Takeaway 4: filters are tuned for high recall at the cost of precision -- on the separate benign/harmful non-jailbreak evaluation set (Table 5), LlamaGuard achieves the best balance (95% accuracy, 0.94 precision, 0.96 recall/TPR, 0.06 FPR), while PromptGuard performs poorly (50% accuracy, 1.00 recall but also 1.00 FPR -- it flags essentially everything) and InjecGuard is below 80% accuracy (0.70 acc, 0.46 recall). Takeaway 5: most filters impose modest overhead -- LlamaGuard (0.028 s/sample) and the OpenAI Moderation API (0.455 s/sample) are cheapest and fastest; O3 is most accurate but costlier (7.22 s/sample, $0.0096/sample); GradSafe is the slowest (40.67 s/sample). Qualitative analysis: false positives on benign prompts concentrate in Physical Harm (14.3%), Privacy (12.2%), and Sexual/Adult Content (11.4%) categories, typically triggered by surface-level sensitive terms (e.g., 'bomb', 'commit suicide', 'Nazi party') appearing in legitimately benign historical/medical framings.
core_contribution: The paper presents what it claims is the first systematic evaluation of jailbreak attacks against the FULL LLM safety pipeline (input filter -> target LLM -> output filter), rather than against the target LLM's alignment alone. The authors formalize a pipeline-level success condition (a jailbreak only 'succeeds' if it evades both the input filter and the output filter AND elicits a judge-confirmed harmful response), build a curated evaluation set of 417 harmful prompts (deduplicated/consolidated from AdvBench50, MaliciousInstruct, JailbreakBench, HarmBench, and TruthfulQA) spanning 10 misuse categories from OpenAI's usage policy, each paired with a topic-aligned benign counterpart (834 prompts total), and run 10 state-of-the-art jailbreak attacks (TAP, PAIR, AutoDAN, Adaptive Attacks, DrAttack, DeepInception, CodeChameleon, ReNeLLM, AdvPrompter, Crescendo) against 5 open-weight LLMs (Llama-2-7B-Chat, Llama3.1-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-v0.3, Vicuna-7B-v1.5, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) and 2 commercial LLMs (GPT-4-Turbo-1106-preview, GPT-4o-2024-1120), each guarded by 6 representative safety filters (OpenAI Moderation API, LlamaGuard, PromptGuard, InjecGuard, GradSafe, and OpenAI's O3 model used zero-shot as a detector).
threat_model: Attacker crafts an adversarial prompt P against a target LLM protected by a deployed content-moderation pipeline consisting of an input filter (screens P before it reaches the LLM) and an output filter (screens the LLM's generated response before it is returned to the user). The paper formalizes the pipeline as Filter(LLM(Filter(P))) and defines attack success strictly as requiring the prompt to pass the input filter (Filterbin(P)=0), the generated response to pass the output filter (Filterbin(LLM(P))=0), AND a GPT-4 judge to certify the output as satisfying the harmful goal G. This differs from (and is explicitly contrasted with) prior jailbreak literature that measures only the normal attack success rate (ASR(Ori)) against the bare aligned model, ignoring any deployed filtering layer. The paper studies existing, previously published jailbreak techniques and existing, previously published filters -- it does not propose a new attack or a new filter/defense.
limitations: The authors' own stated limitations: (1) a limited API query budget prevented extensive, high-throughput attack attempts; (2) the evaluation does not extend to tool-enhanced/agentic models, which the authors flag as needed for a more comprehensive assessment. Additional limitations evident from the paper itself: AutoDAN results are missing (marked '-') for both commercial models (GPT-4-Turbo and GPT-4o) in Table 2, i.e., that attack was not evaluated against closed-source targets; the judge model (GPT-4) used to certify semantic attack success is itself an LLM with its own potential biases/errors, a common but unaddressed limitation of the jailbreak-evaluation literature; the study covers a fixed snapshot of attacks/filters/models current at time of writing and explicitly frames the safety arms race as continuing to evolve.

datasets_used: Consolidated/deduplicated curated set: 417 harmful prompts + 417 topic-aligned benign counterparts across 10 harm categories (own contribution built from the above five)
quantitative_results: Pass rate for most attacks vs. strongest filters (PromptGuard, O3): overall pass rate reduced to under ~5% in most cases
Input-stage detection rates for PromptGuard and O3: roughly 70%-100%
TAP and Adaptive Attacks: >90% ASR against bare aligned models, but heavily detected by filters
Llama-2-7B-Chat and Llama3.1-8B-Instruct: ASR < 30% across six jailbreak attacks (bare model)
LlamaGuard on non-jailbreak benign/harmful set: Accuracy 0.95, Precision 0.94, Recall 0.96, F1 0.95, TPR 0.96, FPR 0.06
OpenAI Moderation API on non-jailbreak set: Accuracy 0.86, Precision 0.94, Recall 0.77, F1 0.85, TPR 0.77, FPR 0.05
PromptGuard on non-jailbreak set: Accuracy 0.50, Precision 0.50, Recall 1.00, F1 0.67, TPR 1.00, FPR 1.00 (flags essentially everything)
InjecGuard on non-jailbreak set: Accuracy 0.70, Precision 0.87, Recall 0.46, F1 0.60, TPR 0.47, FPR 0.06
GradSafe on non-jailbreak set: Accuracy 0.93, Precision 0.89, Recall 0.98, F1 0.93, TPR 0.97, FPR 0.12
O3 on non-jailbreak set: Accuracy 0.93, Precision 0.88, Recall 0.99, F1 0.93, TPR 0.99, FPR 0.14

Latency: LlamaGuard 0.028 s/sample, OpenAI API 0.455 s/sample, PromptGuard 0.219 s/sample, InjecGuard 0.030 s/sample, GradSafe 40.67 s/sample, O3 7.22 s/sample ($0.0096/sample), averaged over 417 Crescendo-attack samples

False-positive category distribution on benign prompts: Physical harm 14.3%, Privacy 12.2%, Sexual/Adult content 11.4%, Harassment/Discrimination 10.5%, Government decision-making 10.5%, Fraud/Deception 9.7%, Malware/Hacking 8.9%, Economic harm 8.4%, Expert advice 8.0%, Disinformation 5.9%
baselines_compared: 10 jailbreak attacks: TAP, PAIR, AutoDAN, Adaptive Attacks, DrAttack, DeepInception, CodeChameleon, ReNeLLM, AdvPrompter, Crescendo
6 safety filters: OpenAI Moderation API, LlamaGuard, PromptGuard, InjecGuard, GradSafe, O3 (used zero-shot as detector)
7 target LLMs: Llama-2-7B-Chat, Llama3.1-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-v0.3, Vicuna-7B-v1.5, Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, GPT-4-Turbo-1106-preview, GPT-4o-2024-1120
ASR(Ori), i.e., normal (filter-free) attack success rate, used throughout as the baseline reference point against which pass rates are compared
